config.title: Berliner Unterwelten: FAQ
config.subtitle: Straight answers on which tour to pick, where each one actually starts, how the ticket window works, and the rules that stop people getting in.

items.a: Berliner Unterwelten e.V. is a registered association that opens, documents and maintains underground structures across Berlin, and runs guided tours into them. It is not a museum you can wander through. Almost every space is entered only on a fixed 90 minute guided tour with one of their guides. The current public tours cost €18, or €13 reduced, and they cover a wartime civilian air-raid bunker under Gesundbrunnen station, the inside of the Humboldthain flak tower, a Cold War civil defence facility at Blochplatz, a 1977 nuclear shelter under Pankstraße station, the brewery cellars and escape tunnel at Bernauer Straße, and the Fichtebunker in Kreuzberg.

items.q: Which Berliner Unterwelten tour should I do?
items.a: For most first-time visitors, Tour 1, Dark Worlds. It goes into a civilian air-raid bunker under Gesundbrunnen station that was left close to how the war ended it, and the guide uses it to explain what a raid did to ordinary Berliners before widening out into roughly 160 years of what Berlin built beneath its own streets. Choose differently only for a reason: go to Bernauer Straße for the escape tunnel if the Wall is why you came, take Tour 3 or the Pankstraße shelter if the Cold War standoff interests you more than the war, and take the Humboldthain flak tower tour if structures interest you more than exhibitions.

items.q: Where do Berliner Unterwelten tours start?
items.a: Not all at the same place, and this is where people go wrong. Tour 1 meets at Brunnenstraße 105, 13355 Berlin, beside the info shop at S+U Gesundbrunnen. Tour 2 meets at the lower platform of the flak tower ruin in Volkspark Humboldthain, signed from the park entrance by the Badstraßenbrücke. Tour 3 meets at Badstraße at the corner of Böttgerstraße, 13357. The Pankstraße shelter tour meets at Badstraße 50, 13357, in front of St. Paul’s church. The escape tunnel tour meets at Brunnenstraße 141, 10115 Berlin, which is at Bernauer Straße, about two kilometres south of the station. The Fichtebunker is in Kreuzberg at Fichtestraße 6, 10967. Check the number on your ticket, because 141 is not 105.

items.q: How far in advance do I need to book Berliner Unterwelten?
items.a: Tickets are sold only through the association’s online shop and they appear about 30 days before the date. There is no ticket desk at the meeting point and no queue you can join on the day, and the number of people on each public tour is limited. Advance tickets are excluded from exchange, refund and return, so the date you pick is the date you have. If your trip is more than a month away, set a reminder for the day your date opens. If it is next week, check now and check again in the morning.

items.q: Are Berliner Unterwelten tours available in English?
items.a: Yes, several of the public tours run in English, alongside German and other languages. Which language runs on which day changes with the season, so the online shop calendar is the only reliable timetable. Do not plan around a tour time quoted in an old review.

items.q: Can you take photos on a Berliner Unterwelten tour?
items.a: No. Photography and filming are not permitted on the tours. If your plan was to come away with pictures of a bunker interior, change the plan before you go. The other rules matter just as much: bags larger than a handbag or a small backpack are not allowed, sturdy footwear is obligatory with no sandals or flip-flops, and animals are not permitted.

items.q: Is there a minimum age for Berliner Unterwelten tours?
items.a: Children under 7 are not admitted, and that is a legal limit rather than a house rule. Between 7 and 13 it is a parent or guardian’s decision, and the association itself recommends the tours from 14 up. Two tours go further: the Humboldthain flak tower tour is 18 and over only, and the Tunnel and Bunker Dresdener Straße tour is 14 and over, with its wading version at 18 and over.

No. The association states plainly that the sites are not accessible because of the bunker structures, with several staircases, no lifts and no chairs available. There are also no visitor toilets on site. If step-free access is what you need in Berlin, plan around surface sites instead.

Around 10°C all year in the bunker sites, and the Fichtebunker sits below 10°C. In a Berlin summer this feels pleasant for the first few minutes and then you want a jacket, so bring a layer even in August. On the flak tower tour you also need closed shoes with a solid sole, and you are issued a safety helmet.

items.q: Does the Humboldthain flak tower tour run all year?
items.a: No. That tour runs April to October and closes for the winter. It is also the strictest on age, at 18 and over with no exceptions. If you are in Berlin between November and March, the park and the hill are still there and free to walk up, but the inside of the tower is shut.

items.q: What can I do near the tour after it finishes?
items.a: If you started at Brunnenstraße 105, Volkspark Humboldthain is about five minutes away and free. The hill is wartime rubble piled over and around the flak tower, and the top gives you a view over the northern city. If you took the escape tunnel tour at Brunnenstraße 141, stay on Bernauer Straße: the Berlin Wall Memorial strip runs for over a kilometre, the documentation centre and tower are free, and the Nordbahnhof mezzanine underneath holds a free exhibition on the sealed stations.

If you have any interest in the war, the Cold War or the Wall, yes. It is one of the few places in Berlin where the history has not been reconstructed or represented for you, and you are standing inside the actual structure. The honest caveats are that it is 90 minutes of heavy material in a cold space with no photographs, that you have to book it properly rather than turn up, and that you should not stack it directly on top of another site about the same period. Put daylight and a coffee in between.
